name: Poem: "Life" — Henry Van Dyke

sourceRefs.label: 2024_q page 68 Q113 (real quoted line, with answer key — found while researching Day 3's Teamwork/Courage topic, stashed and used here)
civilserviceaspirants.in - dedicated MCQ set, figures of speech per line

researchNotes: One real confirmed line (stashed from Day-3 research, when this poem's authorship was tested but not yet authored) plus a 6-question web-sourced set. This poem's figure-of-speech labelling leans on some less-common devices already seen rarely in this syllabus (Litotes, Symbolism) — worth extra attention. Expanded 2026-07 pass: sourced the poem's full text for the first time, revealing it's a complete Italian (Petrarchan) sonnet (8-line octave + 6-line sestet) — added its structure, rhyme scheme, the octave-to-sestet turn, and closing-line meaning, none of which the original pass (built only from a handful of quoted lines) had covered.

reading.intro: A reflective poem about living life with courage, purpose, and without regret or fear of the future. Real exam evidence confirms a specific quoted line's authorship; web research fills in the figure-of-speech detail, including two devices (Litotes, Symbolism) not seen elsewhere in this syllabus.

qa.question: Real exam: "Still seeking what I sought when but a boy, new friendship, high adventure and a crown." Who wrote this?
qa.answer: Henry Van Dyke, in "Life" — confirmed via a real TNPSC question testing this exact line's authorship against three other poet options.

qa.question: What is the poem's central theme?
qa.answer: Living life forward-facing, with courage and clear purpose — neither rushing nor dragging, not mourning losses, and not holding back out of fear of an unknown future.

qa.question: Who was Henry Van Dyke?
qa.answer: An American author (1852-1933) — earned degrees from Princeton, served over 20 years as a Presbyterian minister, then returned to Princeton as a professor of English for nearly 20 years.

qa.question: Identify the figure of speech: "Let me but live my life from year to year."
qa.answer: Repetition (the phrase structure repeats a forward-moving, cyclical idea) — cited as Repetition rather than the other options in a real-style question.

qa.question: Identify the figure of speech: "Not mourning for the things that disappear."
qa.answer: Litotes — an understatement that affirms something by negating its opposite (saying 'not mourning' to actually emphasize calm acceptance, rather than stating the acceptance directly).

qa.question: Identify the figure of speech: "So let the way wind up the hill or down."
qa.answer: Personification — 'the way' (the path of life) is treated as something that can actively 'wind', given an active, almost willful quality.

qa.question: Which phrase in the poem is the clearest example of Symbolism?
qa.answer: "The road's last turn" — symbolizing the end of life/journey, rather than a literal physical road.

qa.question: What is the poem's overall structure?
qa.answer: An Italian (Petrarchan) sonnet: an 8-line octave (rhyme scheme abbaabba) followed by a 6-line sestet (a cdcdcd-style variant), 14 lines total.
qa.tip: Italian/Petrarchan sonnet = octave (8) + sestet (6); English/Shakespearean sonnet = three quatrains (4+4+4) + a couplet (2) — both are 14 lines, but the internal grouping differs.

qa.question: What does the octave express, and what turn does the sestet make?
qa.answer: The octave states the wish to live with a 'forward face,' neither rushing to nor turning from life's goal, not mourning losses, and not fearing the future. The sestet turns to affirm that 'the journey will be joy' regardless of whether the path is rough or smooth, ending on hope for the road's 'last turn.'

qa.question: What does the opening line "with forward face and unreluctant soul" establish?
qa.answer: An eager, willing orientation toward moving forward through life — set immediately as the poem's guiding attitude from its very first line.

qa.question: What does "pays its toll to Youth and Age" mean?
qa.answer: That a 'whole and happy heart' gives its due (attention and cheer) to both the youthful and aging phases of life alike, favoring neither stage over the other.

qa.question: What is the poem's final line, and what does it express?
qa.answer: "And hope the road's last turn will be the best" — closing on hopeful anticipation that life's end will be its finest moment, rather than something to dread.

questions.prompt: "Still seeking what I sought when but a boy, new friendship, high adventure and a crown" was written by:
questions.explanation: Henry Van Dyke — confirmed via a real TNPSC question.

questions.prompt: "Let me but live my life from year to year" is cited as an example of:
"Not mourning for the things that disappear" is an example of:
"So let the way wind up the hill or down" uses which figure of speech?
questions.explanation: Personification — the 'way' given the active quality of winding.

questions.prompt: Which phrase is the clearest example of Symbolism in the poem?
questions.explanation: "The road's last turn" symbolizes life's end.

questions.prompt: Henry Van Dyke, in addition to being a poet, spent over 20 years working as a:
questions.explanation: A Presbyterian minister, before returning to Princeton as an English professor.

questions.prompt: "Life" is structured as which type of sonnet?
questions.trick: Italian sonnet = 8+6 line split (octave+sestet); English sonnet = 4+4+4+2 (three quatrains + couplet) — check where the rhyme/thematic break falls.

questions.prompt: The octave (first 8 lines) of "Life" follows which rhyme scheme?

options: A forward-facing, fearless attitude toward living
questions.explanation: A forward-facing, fearless attitude toward living.

questions.prompt: The sestet of "Life" makes what turn from the octave?
options: It affirms the journey will be joy regardless of the path, ending in hope
It shifts to describing nature imagery only
It introduces a new, unrelated topic
questions.explanation: It affirms the journey will be joy, ending on hope for the 'last turn.'

questions.prompt: The poem's opening phrase "forward face and unreluctant soul" establishes:
options: An eager, willing orientation toward the future
questions.explanation: An eager, willing orientation toward the future.

options: Hope that life's end will be its best moment
questions.explanation: Hope that life's end (the 'road's last turn') will be its best moment.

questions.prompt: "Pays its toll to Youth and Age" means the happy heart:
options: Gives due attention/cheer to both youth and old age alike
questions.explanation: Gives due attention/cheer to both youth and old age alike.

questions.prompt: How many total lines does the sonnet "Life" have?
questions.explanation: 14 lines (8-line octave + 6-line sestet), as in any sonnet.

questions.prompt: The sestet of "Life" (last 6 lines) follows a rhyme scheme most commonly cited as:
questions.explanation: A cdcdcd-style variant, distinct from the octave's abbaabba.
